questions.prompt: In K-NN:

options.text: A higher value of the K parameter => higher confidence
A higher value of the K parameter => lower confidence
Higher distance to k nearest instances => higher confidence
Higher distance to k nearest instances => lower confidence

questions.answer: Higher distance to k nearest instances => lower confidence
questions.explanation: In k-NN, greater distance from the nearest neighbors means the local evidence is weaker, so the prediction is less confident.
questions.correctAnswer: Higher distance to k nearest instances => lower confidence

questions.prompt: In kNN, for measuring distance with continuous features we use _____
questions.explanation: Euclidean distance is the standard choice for k-NN when features are continuous-valued.

questions.prompt: How is the performance of k-NN affected by large values of k?

options.text: k-NN becomes sensitive to noise data
Not affected at all. k-NN is robust to any values of k
Large values of k produce the best results

questions.explanation: Very large k oversmooths the decision rule, so the majority class tends to dominate predictions.

questions.prompt: KNN is not suitable when you have ...
questions.explanation: With very many features, distance measures become less informative, which hurts k-NN performance.

questions.prompt: The k in k-nearest neighbour is ...

options.text: The number of neighbours used for computing output
The number of features used to compare neighbours
The number of features used to set the distance
The number of neighbours used to cluster data

questions.answer: The number of neighbours used for computing output
questions.explanation: In k-NN, k is the number of neighbors used to make the prediction.
questions.correctAnswer: The number of neighbours used for computing output

studyTips: Remember: k-NN depends on distance, so scaling is critical.
k=1 often memorizes noise and overfits.
Categorical features must be encoded before using distance-based models.

topics.title: Regression and cross-validation
topics.description: Regression goals, linear regression, coefficients, k-fold CV, model selection, MSE, and multicollinearity.

questions.prompt: What is the main goal of regression?
Which of the following is a regression task?
What does a linear regression model assume?
What is k-fold cross-validation?
Why is cross-validation useful?
What is a drawback of cross-validation?
You add many irrelevant features to a regression model. What is the likely effect?
questions.explanation: Correct answer: Model may overfit noise

questions.prompt: You compare two models using cross-validation. Model A has stable scores, Model B has highly varying scores. What does this suggest?
questions.explanation: Correct answer: Model B is unstable

questions.prompt: You use k-fold cross-validation with k=2. What is a drawback?
You use cross-validation to choose between models. What is the main goal?
You fit regression with highly correlated features and notice large coefficient values. Why?
You apply linear regression without intercept on shifted data. What happens?
What does this code measure?mean_squared_error(y_test, y_pred)
What does this pipeline help prevent?pipe = Pipeline([ ('scaler', StandardScaler()), ('model', LinearRegression())])cross_val_score(pipe, X, y, cv=5)
What does this code display?pd.DataFrame({ 'feature': X.columns, 'coef': model.coef_})
What does this code return? scores = cross_val_score(model, X, y, cv=5, scoring='r2')
questions.explanation: cross_val_score with scoring='r2' returns one R² score for each fold, not a trained model.

questions.prompt: You increase training data size significantly. What is expected?
questions.explanation: With more representative training data, models usually generalize better because they see a broader sample of the true pattern.

questions.prompt: In a simple regression model, if you increase the input value by 1 then you expect the output to change by:

options.text: The value of the slope parameter
The value of the intercept parameter
The values of the slope AND the intercept parameters

questions.answer: The value of the slope parameter
questions.explanation: In simple linear regression, increasing x by 1 changes the prediction by the slope coefficient.
questions.correctAnswer: The value of the slope parameter

questions.prompt: What does the least squares method do?

options.text: Finds the best values for intercept and slope that provide us the smallest residual sum of squares
Finds the least problematic regression line
Finds the best values for intercept and slope that provide us the greatest residual sum of squares
Finds the best values for intercept and slope that provide us the smallest residual

questions.answer: Finds the best values for intercept and slope that provide us the smallest residual sum of squares
questions.explanation: Least squares chooses coefficients that minimize the residual sum of squares.
questions.correctAnswer: Finds the best values for intercept and slope that provide us the smallest residual sum of squares

questions.prompt: Suppose you are using k-fold cross-validation to assess model quality. How many times should you train the model?
questions.explanation: In k-fold cross-validation, the model is trained once per fold, so k times in total.

studyTips: Regression predicts numeric values.
Cross-validation gives a more stable estimate than one split.
Watch out for multicollinearity when coefficients become unstable.

topics.title: Regularization: Ridge, LASSO, Elastic Net
topics.description: L1/L2 penalties, lambda strength, high-dimensional overfitting, and coefficient shrinkage.

questions.prompt: Which method combines LASSO and Ridge?
What does this code do?Ridge(alpha=1.0)
What does this code do?Lasso(alpha=0.1)
Why is regularization useful in high-dimensional data?
What does λ (lambda) control in regularization?
What happens when λ is very large?
What happens when λ = 0?
Which formula represents LASSO regression loss?
questions.explanation: LASSO adds an L1 penalty, so its objective is MSE plus lambda times the sum of absolute coefficient values.

studyTips: Ridge = L2, LASSO = L1, Elastic Net = both.
Large lambda shrinks coefficients more strongly.
Regularization mainly fights overfitting.

questions.prompt: What does accuracy measure?
questions.explanation: Correct answer: All correct predictions ratio

questions.prompt: Given TP=50, TN=40, FP=10, FN=0, what is accuracy?
What is precision?

options.text: TP divided by all actual positives

questions.explanation: Correct answer: TP divided by predicted positives

questions.prompt: What function is used in logistic regression?
What is the sigmoid function output range?
What is the decision threshold in logistic regression?
What is AUC?

AUC close to 1 means?
What happens if FP increases?
What happens if FN increases?
Given confusion matrix [[50,10],[5,35]], what is accuracy?
You run logistic regression and get this warning:ConvergenceWarning: lbfgs failed to convergeWhat is the most likely fix?
questions.explanation: Correct answer: Increase max_iter or scale features

questions.prompt: What is the issue in this code?model = LogisticRegression()model.fit(X_train, y_train)print(model.coef_)Features are on very different scales

options.text: No issue, coefficients are always comparable
Coefficients are not comparable without scaling

questions.answer: Coefficients are not comparable without scaling
questions.explanation: Correct answer: Coefficients are not comparable without scaling
questions.correctAnswer: Coefficients are not comparable without scaling

questions.prompt: You run logistic regression on perfectly separable data. What happens?
questions.explanation: Correct answer: Coefficients may grow very large

questions.prompt: What is the issue here?model = LogisticRegression(penalty='l1')model.fit(X_train, y_train)

options.text: L1 cannot be used with classification

questions.explanation: Correct answer: Solver must support L1 penalty

questions.prompt: You run this code:model = LogisticRegression()model.fit(X_train, y_train)y_pred = (model.predict_proba(X_test)[:,1] > 0.3)What is the main effect?
What is the issue here? Dataset is highly imbalanced model.fit(X_train, y_train)y_pred = model.predict(X_test)accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred)
What does this code do? LogisticRegression(class_weight='balanced')
What happens if distance threshold is very small?
You use PCA for classification but accuracy drops. What is the likely reason?
Given precision=0.5 and recall=0.5, what is F1-score?
questions.explanation: When precision and recall are both 0.5, their harmonic mean is also 0.5.

questions.prompt: What happens when threshold decreases?
questions.explanation: Lowering the classification threshold makes it easier to predict the positive class, so more samples are labeled positive.

questions.prompt: Given confusion matrix [[50,10],[5,35]], what is precision?
questions.explanation: Using the exam's confusion-matrix convention, precision is 50 / (50 + 10) = 0.83.

questions.prompt: What is wrong in this multi-class code? model = LogisticRegression() model.fit(X_train, y_train) y_pred = model.predict_proba(X_test)[:,1]

options.text: Only one class probability is taken

questions.answer: Only one class probability is taken
questions.explanation: For multi-class problems, taking only `[:,1]` keeps one class probability and ignores the rest of the probability matrix.
questions.correctAnswer: Only one class probability is taken

questions.prompt: In the logistic function y = 1/(1 + e^(-z)), where z = b0 + b1*x1 + b2*x2 + ... + bk*xk, what is k?

options.text: sets of values of respective Xs and Ys

questions.explanation: In the linear term z = b0 + b1*x1 + ... + bk*xk, k is the number of feature variables included in the model.

questions.prompt: In confusion matrix for cancer detection if we predicted yes, but they don't have the disease
questions.explanation: A false positive means the model predicted the positive class even though the true class was negative.

questions.prompt: This technique associates a conditional probability value with each data instance.
questions.explanation: Logistic regression models class probability, so it naturally outputs a conditional probability for each instance.

questions.prompt: Which algorithm is not suitable for solving a problem in which objects need to be divided into classes?
questions.explanation: Linear regression predicts continuous values, so it is not the right algorithm for class labels.

studyTips: Precision focuses on predicted positives; recall focuses on actual positives.
AUC near 0.5 is random; near 1 is strong.
Lower threshold usually increases recall.

questions.answer: Validation is done on holdout data

questions.correctAnswer: Validation is done on holdout data

questions.prompt: What split is preferred in decision trees?
A node has entropy 1. After split, weighted entropy is 0.4. What is information gain?
If a split does not change entropy, what happens?
What happens if all splits give low information gain?
What is the effect of noisy data on entropy-based splits?
questions.explanation: Correct answer: Tree may overfit noise

questions.prompt: What happens if max_depth is not limited in entropy-based trees?
What happens if tree grows without limits?
Which is a hyperparameter in decision trees?
What does each tree in Random Forest use?
questions.explanation: Correct answer: Random subset of data

questions.prompt: What does a decision tree split aim to achieve?
questions.explanation: A decision-tree split tries to create child nodes that separate target values more clearly than the parent node.

questions.prompt: What is the formula for entropy?
questions.explanation: Entropy is defined as -Σ p log p and measures impurity in a node.

questions.prompt: What is the practical effect of increasing min_samples_leaf?
questions.explanation: Increasing `min_samples_leaf` prevents tiny terminal nodes and usually makes the tree smoother and less prone to overfitting.

questions.prompt: A fundamental topic in ML is bias, variance and their relationship with learning algorithms. High variance models typically pay a lot of attention to the training data and don't generalize well to unseen data. Low variance models focus on patterns that will later generalize well to unseen data. Which of the following algorithms can be considered high variance models?
questions.explanation: A single decision tree is a high-variance model because small data changes can produce a very different tree.

studyTips: Unlimited trees can overfit easily.
Information gain = parent entropy - weighted child entropy.
Use validation/test scores, not only training score.

topics.title: Imbalanced data handling
topics.description: Undersampling, oversampling, SMOTE, class weights, misleading accuracy, F1 and recall.

questions.prompt: What is a drawback of undersampling?
What is a drawback of oversampling?
What does SMOTE do?
What is the correct fix for imbalanced evaluation?
questions.explanation: Correct answer: Use F1 or recall

questions.prompt: What would be better scoring for imbalanced classification?
questions.explanation: On imbalanced classification tasks, F1 is usually more informative than regression metrics because it balances precision and recall.

questions.prompt: What happens if recall is low?
questions.explanation: Low recall means the model is missing many actual positive cases, which corresponds to more false negatives.

questions.prompt: Let's assume you are working with a severely imbalanced dataset. You want to split the data into 2 categories using a classification learning algorithm. Which of the following metrics should you avoid using when analysing the algorithm's performance?
questions.explanation: Accuracy can look deceptively high on severely imbalanced data even when the minority class is predicted poorly.

studyTips: Accuracy can lie when classes are imbalanced.
Use recall/F1 when minority class matters.
Oversampling can overfit; undersampling can lose information.

questions.prompt: You run KMeans with this code:kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters=3, random_state=42)labels = kmeans.fit_predict(X_scaled)You notice one cluster has only 2 points while others have thousands. What does this suggest?

questions.explanation: Correct answer: Large distances indicate anomalies

questions.prompt: Optimal number of clusters in silhouette method is:
questions.explanation: Correct answer: Maximizing average silhouette score

questions.prompt: What is inertia in KMeans?
questions.explanation: KMeans inertia is the within-cluster sum of squared distances from points to their assigned centroids.

questions.prompt: You choose K using elbow method, but the curve is smooth with no clear elbow. What should you do?

questions.prompt: What does hyperparameter tuning do?

Expands the parameter set of a model to improve performance

questions.prompt: What does a decision tree split aim to achieve?
questions.explanation: A decision-tree split tries to create child nodes that separate target values more clearly than the parent node.

questions.prompt: What is the formula for entropy?
questions.explanation: Entropy is defined as -Σ p log p and measures impurity in a node.

questions.prompt: What would be better scoring for imbalanced classification?
questions.explanation: On imbalanced classification tasks, F1 is usually more informative than regression metrics because it balances precision and recall.

questions.prompt: What is the practical effect of increasing min_samples_leaf?
questions.explanation: Increasing `min_samples_leaf` prevents tiny terminal nodes and usually makes the tree smoother and less prone to overfitting.

questions.prompt: What happens if recall is low?
questions.explanation: Low recall means the model is missing many actual positive cases, which corresponds to more false negatives.

questions.prompt: What is inertia in KMeans?
questions.explanation: KMeans inertia is the within-cluster sum of squared distances from points to their assigned centroids.

questions.prompt: You choose K using elbow method, but the curve is smooth with no clear elbow. What should you do?
questions.explanation: If the elbow is unclear, the elbow method is inconclusive, so another metric such as silhouette score is a better guide.

questions.prompt: You scale data and run KMeans. Then someone suggests removing scaling because “units are meaningful”. What is the correct reasoning?

options.text: Scaling choice depends on distance meaning

questions.answer: Scaling choice depends on distance meaning
questions.explanation: Because KMeans is distance-based, scaling should depend on whether the feature units should influence distance.
questions.correctAnswer: Scaling choice depends on distance meaning

questions.prompt: What happens if distance threshold is very large?
questions.explanation: A larger distance threshold allows more merges, so you end up with fewer final clusters.

questions.prompt: You compare ward vs complete linkage. What differs most?
questions.explanation: Ward and complete linkage often differ most in the shapes of clusters they tend to create.

questions.prompt: What happens if LDA is applied without labels?
questions.explanation: LDA is a supervised dimensionality-reduction method, so it cannot be fit without class labels.

questions.prompt: You increase n_components and reconstruction error decreases. What does this mean?
questions.explanation: Adding more NMF components usually allows a closer reconstruction of the original matrix, which lowers reconstruction error.

questions.category: Ensemble learning: Random Forest, AdaBoost, Gradient Boosting
questions.prompt: What is the main benefit of Random Forest?
questions.explanation: Random Forest averages many de-correlated trees, which reduces variance and helps control overfitting.

questions.prompt: What is a cluster prototype?

questions.answer: A representative object of a cluster
questions.explanation: A cluster prototype is a representative object or summary point for a cluster.

questions.prompt: What is complete linkage?

options.text: Distance between farthest points of clusters

questions.answer: Distance between farthest points of clusters
questions.explanation: Complete linkage defines inter-cluster distance using the farthest pair of points across the two clusters.

questions.prompt: What is single linkage?

options.text: Distance between closest points of clusters

questions.answer: Distance between closest points of clusters

questions.correctAnswer: Distance between closest points of clusters

questions.prompt: What is divisive clustering?
questions.explanation: Divisive clustering starts with one large cluster and repeatedly splits it into smaller ones.

questions.prompt: What does silhouette coefficient measure?
questions.explanation: The silhouette coefficient measures how well points fit their own cluster compared with neighboring clusters.

questions.prompt: Which method is more complex to implement?
questions.explanation: Divisive clustering is typically harder to implement because it requires repeated splitting decisions.

questions.prompt: What happens after assigning points to clusters?
questions.explanation: After assigning points to the nearest clusters, KMeans recomputes centroids as the mean of the assigned points.

questions.prompt: Which distance is commonly used in K-means?
questions.explanation: Standard KMeans is based on Euclidean distance.

questions.prompt: What is the complexity of K-means dependent on?

options.text: Number of points, clusters, and iterations

questions.answer: Number of points, clusters, and iterations
questions.explanation: The time cost of KMeans grows with the number of points, clusters, and iterations it must process.
questions.correctAnswer: Number of points, clusters, and iterations

questions.prompt: What happens if K is chosen incorrectly?
questions.explanation: If K is too small or too large, the partition will not match the underlying structure well.

questions.prompt: K-means assumes clusters are:
questions.explanation: KMeans works best when clusters are roughly spherical around their centroids.

questions.prompt: What is a major weakness of K-means:
questions.explanation: Because KMeans starts from initial centroids, different initializations can lead to different local optima.

questions.prompt: In agglomerative hierarchical clustering, what is the initial step?

options.text: Each object is its own cluster

questions.prompt: What is the stopping condition in K-means?
questions.explanation: KMeans typically stops when centroid locations or cluster assignments stop changing meaningfully.

questions.prompt: What is the first step in K-means algorithm?
questions.explanation: The first step in KMeans is to initialize cluster centroids.

questions.prompt: What is a centroid ?
questions.explanation: A centroid is the mean location of the points assigned to a cluster.

questions.prompt: K-means is sensitive to:
questions.explanation: Outliers can pull centroids away from dense regions, so KMeans is sensitive to them.

questions.prompt: K-means mainly works with which type of data?
questions.explanation: KMeans relies on numeric distance calculations, so it mainly works with numeric feature vectors.

questions.prompt: What does K in K-means represent?
questions.explanation: In KMeans, K is the number of clusters the algorithm should form.

questions.prompt: What is vector quantization in clustering?

questions.answer: A way to compress data using cluster prototypes
questions.explanation: Vector quantization compresses data by replacing points with representative cluster prototypes.

options.text: Detects clusters based on density and identifies noise

questions.prompt: What is a limitation of agglomerative clustering?
questions.explanation: Once agglomerative clustering merges two clusters, that earlier decision cannot be undone.

questions.prompt: What does Ward's method minimize?
questions.explanation: Ward's method chooses merges that minimize the increase in within-cluster sum of squares.

questions.prompt: What is hierarchical clustering?

questions.explanation: KMeans is prototype-based because each cluster is represented by a centroid prototype.

questions.prompt: How are points assigned to clusters?
questions.explanation: Each point is assigned to the cluster with the nearest centroid.

questions.prompt: Suppose you are using k-fold cross-validation to assess model quality. How many times should you train the model?
questions.explanation: In k-fold cross-validation, the model is trained once per fold, so k times in total.

questions.prompt: This technique associates a conditional probability value with each data instance.

questions.prompt: Let's assume you are working with a severely imbalanced dataset. You want to split the data into 2 categories using a classification learning algorithm. Which of the following metrics should you avoid using when analysing the algorithm's performance?
questions.explanation: Accuracy can look deceptively high on severely imbalanced data even when the minority class is predicted poorly.

questions.explanation: A single decision tree is a high-variance model because small data changes can produce a very different tree.
